title: Escovitch fish
subtitle: Fried fish in a sharp vinegar-and-pepper escabeche
excerpt: Whole fish or thick fillets fried crisp, then drenched while still hot with a sharp pickled sauce of onion, carrot, scotch bonnet, and vinegar. Eaten at room temperature, the escabeche permeates the flesh.
sourceNotes: Escovitch is the Jamaican term for escabeche, a technique of pickling fried fish in vinegar brought to Jamaica via Spanish colonisers in the sixteenth century. The Jamaican version typically uses whole red snapper or porgy, fried crisp in hot oil and immediately submerged in a peppery vinegar sauce with sliced scotch bonnet, onion, carrot, and allspice. It is characteristic beach and roadside food in Jamaica. This recipe uses cod fillet as a widely available substitute for red snapper. Note: red snapper and other whole fish may need to be added to the ingredient table.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The escabeche sauce can be made up to 2 days ahead. Fry the fish on the day and pour over the sauce while hot.

content.text: The logic of escovitch is the same as escabeche: fry fish until it is properly crisp, then pour a hot vinegared sauce over the top while the fish is still sizzling. The vinegar penetrates the flesh as the fish cools, pickling it lightly from the outside in. The result is eaten at room temperature or slightly warm, the acidity of the sauce cutting through the richness of the fried fish.

In Jamaica, whole red snapper is the classic choice. Cod fillet works well as a substitute and is widely available in the UK. Whatever fish you use, it should be very fresh, well-dried before flouring, and fried in hot oil so the exterior crisps rather than steams. A limp, oily escovitch is the result of fish going into oil that isn't hot enough.

items.prepNote: thick fillets, or use whole red snapper if available
thinly sliced, seeds retained for heat

content.text: Combine the {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar, {{allspice}} of allspice, and {{peppercorns-black}} whole black peppercorns in a small saucepan with 50 ml of water. Bring to a simmer and cook for 5 minutes. Add the sliced {{onion}} onion rings, {{pepper-green}} green pepper, and {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Simmer for 3 minutes — the vegetables should wilt but retain some bite. Keep hot over a very low heat.

Pat the {{cod-fillet}} fish fillets completely dry with kitchen paper. Season the {{plain-flour}} of flour with salt and pepper on a flat plate. Dust each fillet evenly in the seasoned flour, shaking off the excess. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat until it shimmers. Fry the fish for 4 minutes per side, turning once, until golden-brown and cooked through. Work in batches if needed to avoid overcrowding.

Transfer the hot fried fish to a shallow dish or plate. Immediately pour the hot escabeche sauce over the fish, making sure the onion and peppers cover the surface. Leave for at least 20–30 minutes before serving; the sauce will permeate the fish as it cools. Serve at room temperature.

items.cause: Oil not hot enough; fish not properly dried before flouring
items.fix: Pat fish very dry and heat the oil until it shimmers before adding. Don't move the fish until it releases naturally from the pan

items.cause: Too much vinegar or not enough sugar
items.fix: Add another teaspoon of dark brown sugar and a splash of water to the sauce. The balance should be acidic but not harsh
Cod fillets should be at least 2.5 cm thick. Remove from the pan as soon as the flesh flakes when pressed at the centre

items.symptom: Vegetables in the sauce too soft
items.fix: Add the sliced vegetables to the hot vinegar liquid for no more than 3 minutes; they should remain slightly crisp to provide texture against the fish

content.text: Escovitch fish traces its lineage to the Spanish escabeche tradition, arriving in Jamaica with the Spanish settlers of the sixteenth century and absorbed into the local cooking culture. In Jamaica it is strongly associated with beach food and with Easter Friday, when fish replaces meat. The roadside stalls serve it in polystyrene boxes with hard dough bread and festival dumplings, the vinegary fish juices soaking into everything. At home, it sits best alongside rice and peas or a simple salad.
